description: Industry-standard cloud migration candidate scoring criteria. Higher overall score = better migration candidate. Edit this file and run reload_migration_criteria to apply changes.

criteria.name: Business Criticality
criteria.description: How critical is this application to business operations? Higher criticality workloads often need careful wave placement — not necessarily early (more risk) or late (more cost). Score is inverted for wave sequencing: start with lower criticality.

criteria.name: Technical Complexity
criteria.description: How complex is the application technically? Assessed from dependency count, age, technology stack, and OS support status. Lower complexity = better early candidate.

bands.label: Very high dependencies — defer or refactor first

criteria.name: Vendor Support Status
criteria.description: Is the application on a vendor-supported platform? End-of-life or unsupported platforms are strong migration drivers but also increase technical risk.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true = supported (score 60), false = EOL (score 100 — strong migration driver)

criteria.name: Cloud Readiness
criteria.description: How ready is the application for cloud deployment? Considers whether source code is available, containerisation feasibility, and modern runtime.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true = source available (score 80), false = binary-only (score 30)

criteria.name: Current Infrastructure Cost
criteria.description: Annual on-premises or hosted cost in USD. Higher cost = stronger business case for migration.

criteria.name: Application Age
criteria.description: Age of the application in years. Older applications may have higher technical debt but also stronger decommission motivation.

bands.label: < 2 years — recently built, may not need migration

criteria.name: SaaS Alternative Availability
criteria.description: Is there a viable SaaS replacement? Presence of SaaS alternative enables Repurchase strategy with potentially lower migration effort.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true = SaaS exists (score 90), false = no SaaS (score 50)

criteria.name: Data Sensitivity
criteria.description: Data classification affects migration complexity. Lower sensitivity = easier migration. High sensitivity adds controls overhead.

criteria.name: Documentation Quality
criteria.description: Well-documented applications are easier to migrate with confidence. Poor documentation increases discovery effort and risk.

criteria.name: Compliance Overhead
criteria.description: Number of regulatory compliance requirements (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, etc.). More requirements add migration complexity and controls overhead.

criteria.name: Application Activity (Zombie/Idle Detection)
criteria.description: Is the workload actively used? AWS Prescriptive Guidance defines zombie workloads as CPU/memory below 5% for 90 days with no inbound connections. Idle workloads are 5-20% utilisation for 90 days. Both are Retire candidates. Source: AWS MAP guidance.

bands.label: Zombie — CPU/memory < 5% for 90 days. Strong Retire candidate.
Idle — CPU/memory 5-20% for 90 days. Evaluate for Retire.
Very high utilisation — may need rightsizing

criteria.name: Architecture Coupling (Code Anti-Patterns)
criteria.description: Presence of code and architecture anti-patterns that complicate migration: hardcoded IPs, local filesystem dependencies, COM/DCOM, Windows Registry coupling. More anti-patterns = lower score. Source: AWS, Azure CAF, IBM, DXC, Uptime Institute 2025 (38% of failures cite undiscovered dependency conflicts).

bands.label: 3 anti-patterns — high remediation effort before migration
4+ anti-patterns — defer or consider Retain/Replatform with major remediation

criteria.name: Hardware Independence
criteria.description: Does the workload require physical hardware (dongles, FPGAs, specialised NICs, proprietary storage)? Hardware dependencies are a hard migration blocker for standard IaaS. Source: Gartner, IBM Rapid Assessment, all cloud providers.
criteria.notes: Boolean: false=100 (no hardware dep, good candidate), true=0 (hard blocker — cannot migrate to standard IaaS)

criteria.name: Latency Requirements
criteria.description: What are the application's latency SLA requirements? Sub-millisecond latency workloads (HFT, industrial control, SCADA) cannot tolerate cloud network overhead and should be Retained. Source: Google Cloud, IBM, DXC.

bands.label: Sub-millisecond — hard blocker, cannot migrate to standard cloud
< 50ms — manageable with cloud Direct Connect / ExpressRoute
No strict latency requirement — cloud suitable

criteria.name: Mainframe / Non-x86 Platform Risk
criteria.description: Is the workload running on a mainframe, AS/400, or other non-x86 platform? These require specialist tooling, extended timelines, and significantly higher effort than standard migrations. Source: IBM, BMC, TCS, DXC, mLogica.
criteria.notes: Boolean: false=80 (not mainframe), true=10 (mainframe — requires specialist assessment, separate programme track)

criteria.name: Cloud Licensing Rights Confirmed
criteria.description: Has the licensing team confirmed that all software licences permit cloud deployment? Some perpetual licences prohibit cloud hosting or require significant uplift. Source: Azure CAF, AWS MAP, Gartner.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=90 (licensing confirmed), false=20 (unconfirmed — do not migrate until resolved)

criteria.name: 12-Factor: Stateless Processes
criteria.description: Application maintains no in-process state between requests. Stateless apps support horizontal scaling and container restarts without data loss. Source: 12factor.net Factor VI; CIS Docker Benchmark; AWS ECS/EKS best practices.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (stateless — excellent container candidate), false=0 (stateful — must externalise state before containerising), undefined=50 (unknown — partial credit)

criteria.name: 12-Factor: Config via Environment Variables
criteria.description: All configuration (URLs, credentials, feature flags) is injected via environment variables rather than baked into code or config files. Source: 12factor.net Factor III; CIS Docker Benchmark 4.1.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (env-var config — 12-factor compliant), false=0 (hardcoded config — refactor required), undefined=50

criteria.name: 12-Factor: No Local Filesystem Persistent State
criteria.description: Application does not write persistent data to the local filesystem. Local writes prevent container scaling and cause data loss on restart. Source: 12factor.net Factor IV; AWS Well-Architected.
criteria.notes: Boolean: false=100 (no local FS state — compliant), true=0 (has local FS dependency — blocker for containerisation), undefined=50

criteria.name: 12-Factor: Health Check Endpoint
criteria.description: Application exposes a health check endpoint (e.g. /health, /readyz) compatible with container liveness and readiness probes. Required for Kubernetes and ECS health management. Source: 12factor.net Factor IX; Azure AKS baseline architecture; CNCF.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (health endpoint present), false=0 (no health endpoint — add before deploying to orchestrator), undefined=50

criteria.name: 12-Factor: Structured Logging to stdout
criteria.description: Application emits structured (JSON) logs to stdout/stderr rather than writing to files or syslog. Container log aggregation pipelines (CloudWatch, Datadog, Splunk) consume stdout. Source: 12factor.net Factor XI; AWS CloudWatch Container Insights.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (structured stdout logging), false=0 (file-based logging — reconfigure before containerising), undefined=50

criteria.name: Non-Root Container Execution
criteria.description: Container runs as a non-root user. Running as root violates CIS Docker Benchmark 4.1 and is blocked by most production Kubernetes admission controllers (OPA, Kyverno, Pod Security Standards). Source: CIS Docker Benchmark 4.1; CNCF; AWS EKS best practices.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (non-root — security compliant), false=0 (root — violates CIS benchmark, blocked by most admission controllers), undefined=50

criteria.name: Dockerfile / Container Image Present
criteria.description: A Dockerfile or equivalent container image definition already exists. Indicates previous containerisation effort and reduces image build effort. Source: CNCF; AWS ECR best practices.
criteria.notes: Boolean: true=100 (Dockerfile exists), false=20 (no Dockerfile — must be created), undefined=50

criteria.name: No Privileged Mode Requirement
criteria.description: Container does not require privileged mode (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, --privileged). Privileged containers bypass all Linux namespace isolation and are rejected by all CIS-compliant Kubernetes clusters. Source: CIS Docker Benchmark 5.4; CNCF security whitepaper.
criteria.notes: Boolean: false=100 (no privileged mode required — compliant), true=0 (requires privileged mode — hard blocker for managed Kubernetes), undefined=50

criteria.name: Container Readiness (12-Factor Compliance)
criteria.description: How ready is the application for containerisation? Assessed against 12-factor app principles: stateless processes, config via environment variables, no local filesystem state, health check endpoint, structured logging, and non-root execution. Directly determines Replatform (containerise) vs Rehost (IaaS VM) strategy eligibility. Source: 12factor.net; CIS Docker Benchmark; CNCF; AWS ECS/EKS best practices; Azure AKS baseline architecture.
criteria.notes: Composite: isStateless=true (+30), configViaEnvVars=true (+20), hasHealthCheckEndpoint=true (+15), hasStructuredLogging=true (+15), runsAsNonRootUser=true (+10), hasDockerfile=true (+10). Max 100. isAlreadyContainerised=true sets score to 85. hasPhysicalHardwareDependency=true or requiresPrivilegedMode=true sets score to 0.
